steps.dialogue: You showed good understanding there. Now let's practice using both strategies. I'll give you different types of problems - some where denominators match, some where numerators match. See if you can figure out which strategy to use, then compare correctly.
Let's start with something familiar. Compare [fraction numerator=3 denominator=10]three tenths[/fraction] and [fraction numerator=7 denominator=10]seven tenths[/fraction].

step.dialogue: Both have tenths. Count the shaded parts.

metadata.description: Briefly highlight shaded parts with numbers

step.dialogue: Same denominator means count numerators. Look: 3 pieces versus 7 pieces. Which gives you more?

metadata.description: Fill in the < symbol automatically

step.dialogue: Watch: Both tenths, so I count. 3 is less than 7, so [fraction numerator=3 denominator=10]three tenths[/fraction] is less than [fraction numerator=7 denominator=10]seven tenths[/fraction].

on_correct.dialogue: Right. [fraction numerator=3 denominator=10]Three tenths[/fraction] is less than [fraction numerator=7 denominator=10]seven tenths[/fraction]. You counted the pieces.

steps.dialogue: Look at these two pairs. Check what matches in each pair. Which pair would you compare by counting pieces?

prompt.text: Click the pair where you count pieces (left pair: 2/7 and 5/7, right pair: 2/7 and 2/9).

step.dialogue: Check the bottom numbers. Which pair has matching denominators?

metadata.description: Briefly glow denominators in each pair

step.dialogue: Left: both sevenths. Right: sevenths and ninths. Which uses counting?

metadata.description: Highlight the left pair

step.dialogue: Same denominator equals count. The left pair both have 7 on bottom.

on_correct.dialogue: Exactly. The left pair has same denominators - both sevenths - so we count.

steps.dialogue: Now compare [fraction numerator=3 denominator=5]three fifths[/fraction] and [fraction numerator=3 denominator=8]three eighths[/fraction].

step.dialogue: Both show 3 pieces. Look at the SIZE of those pieces.

metadata.description: Pulse the 3 fifths and 3 eighths to show size difference

step.dialogue: Fifths are bigger pieces than eighths. So 3 fifths gives you more than 3 eighths.

metadata.description: Highlight 3/5 as the answer

step.dialogue: Same numerator means compare sizes. Fifths are greater than eighths, so [fraction numerator=3 denominator=5]three fifths[/fraction] is greater than [fraction numerator=3 denominator=8]three eighths[/fraction].

on_correct.dialogue: Yes. [fraction numerator=3 denominator=5]Three fifths[/fraction] is greater because fifths are bigger pieces than eighths.

steps.dialogue: Here's another problem. The right bar shows [fraction numerator=4 denominator=5]four fifths[/fraction]. Create a fraction with fifths that is less than [fraction numerator=4 denominator=5]four fifths[/fraction].

prompt.text: Shade parts to make a fraction less than 4/5.

step.dialogue: We need LESS than [fraction numerator=4 denominator=5]four fifths[/fraction]. Try shading fewer parts.

metadata.description: Highlight parts 0, 1, 2 as possible choices

step.dialogue: Less than [fraction numerator=4 denominator=5]four fifths[/fraction] means fewer pieces. Try shading 1, 2, or 3 sections.

metadata.description: Automatically shade 2 sections

step.dialogue: To make a fraction less than [fraction numerator=4 denominator=5]four fifths[/fraction], we need fewer than 4 pieces. Let's shade 2 sections - that makes [fraction numerator=2 denominator=5]two fifths[/fraction], which is less than [fraction numerator=4 denominator=5]four fifths[/fraction].

on_correct.dialogue: Nice! You created a correct comparison. You understand how this works.

steps.dialogue: Create two fractions with sixths where the first is LESS than the second.

prompt.text: Shade bars to model a comparison where the top fraction is less than the bottom fraction.

step.dialogue: Is your top fraction smaller than the bottom?

metadata.description: Highlight the top bar as needing fewer parts

step.dialogue: We need fewer shaded parts on top than on bottom.

metadata.description: Shade 2 sections on top bar and 4 sections on bottom bar

step.dialogue: Let's work through this together: If I shade 2 on top and 4 on bottom, then [fraction numerator=2 denominator=6]two sixths[/fraction] is less than [fraction numerator=4 denominator=6]four sixths[/fraction].

on_correct.dialogue: Nice! You built your own comparison. You understand how this works.

steps.dialogue: Here are three fractions. Which is the smallest?

step.dialogue: Compare them two at a time. What matches in each pair?

metadata.description: Highlight each comparison pair

step.dialogue: [fraction numerator=4 denominator=9]Four ninths[/fraction] vs [fraction numerator=4 denominator=6]four sixths[/fraction]: ninths are smaller pieces. [fraction numerator=4 denominator=9]Four ninths[/fraction] vs [fraction numerator=7 denominator=9]seven ninths[/fraction]: 4 is less than 7.

metadata.description: Highlight 4/9 as the answer

step.dialogue: [fraction numerator=4 denominator=9]Four ninths[/fraction] is smallest because: ninths are smaller than sixths (so [fraction numerator=4 denominator=9]four ninths[/fraction] is less than [fraction numerator=4 denominator=6]four sixths[/fraction]), and 4 pieces is less than 7 pieces (so [fraction numerator=4 denominator=9]four ninths[/fraction] is less than [fraction numerator=7 denominator=9]seven ninths[/fraction]).

on_correct.dialogue: Right! [fraction numerator=4 denominator=9]Four ninths[/fraction] is smallest. You compared [fraction numerator=4 denominator=9]four ninths[/fraction] to [fraction numerator=4 denominator=6]four sixths[/fraction] using piece sizes, and [fraction numerator=4 denominator=9]four ninths[/fraction] to [fraction numerator=7 denominator=9]seven ninths[/fraction] by counting.

steps.dialogue: Quick check - [fraction numerator=5 denominator=9]five ninths[/fraction] versus [fraction numerator=3 denominator=9]three ninths[/fraction].

step.dialogue: Both have ninths - same size pieces. Count the shaded parts.

metadata.description: Highlight the shaded parts in each bar

step.dialogue: Same denominator means same size pieces. Now count: 5 ninths versus 3 ninths. Which is more?

metadata.description: Highlight 5/9 as the answer

step.dialogue: Both have ninths - that's the same denominator, same size pieces. Now we count the pieces: 5 is greater than 3, so [fraction numerator=5 denominator=9]five ninths[/fraction] is greater than [fraction numerator=3 denominator=9]three ninths[/fraction].

on_correct.dialogue: Yes! Both have ninths - same size pieces. So we count: 5 pieces is more than 3 pieces.

steps.dialogue: Now compare [fraction numerator=5 denominator=6]five sixths[/fraction] and [fraction numerator=5 denominator=10]five tenths[/fraction].

step.dialogue: Both show 5 pieces. Which pieces are bigger - sixths or tenths?

metadata.description: Show visual hint comparing piece sizes

step.dialogue: Think about cutting: Fewer parts in the whole means bigger pieces. Are sixths or tenths bigger?

metadata.description: Show stacked bars with 5/6 and 5/10 shaded

step.dialogue: Both have 5 pieces. Cutting into 6 parts makes bigger pieces than cutting into 10 parts. So sixths are bigger pieces, which means [fraction numerator=5 denominator=6]five sixths[/fraction] is greater than [fraction numerator=5 denominator=10]five tenths[/fraction].

on_correct.dialogue: Right. [fraction numerator=5 denominator=6]Five sixths[/fraction] is greater - sixths are bigger pieces than tenths.

steps.dialogue: Let's put everything together. First, complete each comparison using the right strategy.

prompt.text: Fill in <, >, or = for each pair. Select the answer that shows all three comparisons correctly.

step.dialogue: Check your comparisons. Which pairs have matching denominators? Which have matching numerators?

metadata.description: Highlight each row showing what matches

step.dialogue: Row 1: both eighths - count. Row 2: both twos - compare sizes. Row 3: both sevenths - count.

metadata.description: Highlight the first answer option as correct

step.dialogue: Let's work through these: [fraction numerator=3 denominator=8]three eighths[/fraction] is less than [fraction numerator=5 denominator=8]five eighths[/fraction] because both have eighths, and 3 is less than 5. Then [fraction numerator=2 denominator=5]two fifths[/fraction] is greater than [fraction numerator=2 denominator=9]two ninths[/fraction] because both have 2 pieces, and fifths are bigger than ninths. Finally [fraction numerator=4 denominator=7]four sevenths[/fraction] is greater than [fraction numerator=3 denominator=7]three sevenths[/fraction] because both have sevenths, and 4 is greater than 3.

steps.dialogue: Use each strategy once - make one comparison by counting, another by size. First, find the pair you'd compare by counting.

prompt.text: Click two fractions to compare by counting.

step.dialogue: That pair has the same numerator. We need same DENOMINATOR for counting. Let's try again.

metadata.description: Briefly glow the denominators

step.dialogue: For counting, we need the same bottom number. Look: [fraction numerator=2 denominator=7]two sevenths[/fraction] and [fraction numerator=6 denominator=7]six sevenths[/fraction] both have 7. Or [fraction numerator=2 denominator=5]two fifths[/fraction] and [fraction numerator=4 denominator=5]four fifths[/fraction] both have 5.

metadata.description: Highlight the first option

step.dialogue: Let's figure this out together: [fraction numerator=2 denominator=7]two sevenths[/fraction] and [fraction numerator=6 denominator=7]six sevenths[/fraction] both have sevenths - same denominator means we count. Let's click those two.

on_correct.dialogue: Good! Those have the same denominator.

steps.dialogue: Now find a pair to compare by size.

prompt.text: Click two fractions to compare by piece size.

step.dialogue: You already used counting. Now find a pair with the same TOP number for size comparison.

metadata.description: Briefly glow the numerators

step.dialogue: For size comparison, we need the same numerator. Look for two fractions with the same top number. [fraction numerator=2 denominator=7]Two sevenths[/fraction] and [fraction numerator=2 denominator=5]two fifths[/fraction] both have 2.

metadata.description: Highlight the first option

step.dialogue: Here's what we need: [fraction numerator=2 denominator=7]two sevenths[/fraction] and [fraction numerator=2 denominator=5]two fifths[/fraction] both have 2 pieces - same numerator. That means we compare the SIZE of those pieces. Click [fraction numerator=2 denominator=7]two sevenths[/fraction] and [fraction numerator=2 denominator=5]two fifths[/fraction].

on_correct.dialogue: Nice, you used both methods. You used counting for same denominators, and size comparison for same numerators.

metadata.description: Support/confidence problem - appears after 2+ incorrect answers

steps.dialogue: Let's do an easier one together. [fraction numerator=1 denominator=4]One fourth[/fraction] and [fraction numerator=3 denominator=4]three fourths[/fraction]. Which has more shaded?

metadata.description: Pulse shaded portions gently to draw attention

step.dialogue: Look at how much is shaded. Which bar has more blue?

metadata.description: Show numbers 1 over the single shaded section and 1, 2, 3 over the three shaded sections

step.dialogue: Let's count together. Top bar: 1 shaded part. Bottom bar: 3 shaded parts. Which is more?

metadata.description: Arrow points from 1/4 to 3/4 with < symbol, auto-advance after 3 seconds

step.dialogue: I'll show you. Both have fourths - same size pieces. Top has 1 piece shaded. Bottom has 3 pieces shaded. 3 is more than 1, so [fraction numerator=3 denominator=4]three fourths[/fraction] is bigger.

metadata.description: Green checkmark appears next to 3/4 bar, numbers 1, 2, 3 briefly appear above the three shaded sections

on_correct.dialogue: There we go. When you're not sure, always check what matches first. These both have fourths, so we count: 3 is greater than 1.

steps.dialogue: Good practice! You've shown you can handle both strategies - counting when denominators match, and comparing sizes when numerators match. You even know how to figure out which strategy to use. Let's see how these strategies connect to bigger ideas!
